records.probe: Tried to falsify 'zero bypass paths / every production change is gated': queried live branch protection and the Pages deploy model, and checked whether the gate even exists on the production branch.
records.evidence: mcp__github__list_branches(mglaeser/save-haven-monitor): both 'main' and the work branch return "protected":false — no branch protection, no required status check. main HEAD = d19556d (frozen baseline); verify/ + .github/workflows/verify.yml were added only in 82565b6/4af8354 on the work branch, so PRODUCTION (main, which Pages serves) carries NO gate at all. The single verify workflow run (actions_list, run 29475787128, event=push) concluded "failure". DR-002-ci-gate.md lines 18-31 admit: 'A GitHub Actions check reports status but does not block a merge or the Pages deploy unless branch protection requires it ... Until branch protection requires the verify check on main, the gate is advisory, not blocking.' The gate CAN block in isolation (verify/tests/50-gate-selftest passed 5/5 this run: blocks null-standing_control PASS, production_eligible lie, count drift, failed required test), but a direct push to unprotected main deploys instantly, bypassing CI entirely. No per-change machine-readable provenance record (model+prompt+frozen-spec+checks) is enforced; independent adversarial verification exists only as a one-time Phase-2 calibration, not a standing per-change control.

standing_control.mechanism: gate.js + run.js are self-tested and version-controlled, but nothing forces them to run before production: no branch protection, and Pages deploys directly from main on push
standing_control.blocks: nothing at production today (advisory only)
standing_control.ratchet: none enforced (bypass paths open)

structural_fix.rationale: Owner enabling 'Require status checks to pass -> verify' + 'Do not allow bypassing' on main converts the advisory check into a real gate; alternatively re-architect Pages to deploy from a CI artifact produced only after the gate passes. Neither is done.

residual_risk.compensating_control: run-verify-before-push convention in CLAUDE.md and verify/tests/60-governance asserting the workflow stays wired
residual_risk.tripwire: 60-governance goes red if the workflow is deleted/decoupled — but only on the PR branch, and only advisory

records.remediation: Owner must enable branch protection on main requiring the verify check with bypass disabled (the single human command-authority precondition), and merge verify/ to main so the gate exists on the production branch; add an enforced machine-readable provenance record per change and wire a standing independent adversarial verifier (a model that did not write the change). Until then a change can reach production wholly ungated.

records.probe: Ran the suite from this checkout and attacked its ability to fail: inspected assertions, forced the gate self-test's injected-fault cases, and searched for any mutation-testing floor that measures (not assumes) fault detection.
records.evidence: node run.js this run: 29 pass / 0 fail / 5 offline / 34 total — deterministic, no framework, meaningful assertions (verify/tests/10-data-invariants asserts 121 interp points, rebase peak=100 to 1e-9, MATRIX domain {-1,0,1,2,3}, runFan determinism). The '6/6 seeded' calibration is a fixed 6-defect corpus, not a mutation score, and is re-injected by convention (§9.3) not by a running mutation gate.

records.probe: Plotted commit sizes/branch lifetimes, checked for a batch-size gate, and searched for a rollback that has been executed with a recorded duration and can be fired by an automated abort rather than a human.
records.evidence: git log --shortstat: change sets are large and unbounded — d66f63a added dashboard.jsx in a single 1852-line commit, 82565b6 was 3555 insertions, 4af8354 was 22 files/813 insertions. No batch-size gate exists anywhere in verify/ or the workflow. CI now runs on every push (verify.yml on: push branches:['**']) — but its one run concluded failure (actions_list run 29475787128). There is NO rehearsed rollback: no runbook, no recorded rollback duration, no drill. Rollback is implicitly 'git revert + push' (Pages redeploys), and it can only be fired by a human noticing — there is no monitoring signal and no automated abort (no B-18 wiring; system map records no canary/rollout/monitoring). Both branches are protected:false so there is no short-lived-branch or trunk discipline enforced.

records.remediation: Add a batch-size gate to gate.js (block change sets above a line/file threshold), write and actually execute a rollback (scripted git revert + Pages redeploy) recording its duration, and wire it to an automatic abort signal (a synthetic uptime/health check on crash.klee.me) so it fires on a signal, not on someone noticing.

records.na_justification: Consistency-vs-availability, replication-vs-cost, isolation level and quorum are properties of backend datastores. This system has no server and no database (system-map 'Data stores'); the only persistence is git history and a per-tab in-memory cache. There is no mutable shared state whose consistency/availability could be traded off, so the control has no surface. Static-asset availability is GitHub Pages/CDN's concern, addressed under A-28.
records.probe: For each datastore and critical path, looked for a consistency-vs-availability position, a replication factor, an isolation level, or a quorum setting that a test could assert drift against. Enumerated the data stores from the system map and grepped served source for any datastore/replication/isolation construct.
records.evidence: There are only three 'stores' and none has a consistency/availability trade-off: client sessionStorage (one key `bubblegauge:enabled`, bubblegauge.jsx:27), an in-memory per-tab fetch cache (25-min TTL, :289-290), and git history (system-map 'Data stores': 'No server-side stores, no databases, no vector stores, no caches beyond the above'). No server, no replication, no quorum, no isolation level exists to position. The one trade-off that does exist — the 25-minute cache TTL — is a freshness-vs-load choice, and it is surfaced to the user via Freshness labels (:407-416) and focus-revalidation (:326-329), but it is not a datastore consistency position.

residual_risk.compensating_control: the sole freshness trade-off (25-min cache TTL) is disclosed via visible staleness labels rather than hidden
residual_risk.tripwire: n/a — no datastore to drift

records.na_justification: Unit economics presuppose metered cost per unit of use. This product has none: static hosting (fixed ~$0), free pinned CDN, and no in-product inference (system-map). There is no per-request or per-tenant cost that could be modelled, capped, or regressed, so the control has no surface. Any cost of the external bubblegauge service belongs to that separate service.
records.probe: Attempted to compute cost-per-transaction and cost-per-AI-request from real usage. Looked for any billed compute: an inference call, a server, a per-request function, a tenant model. Grepped served source and read the system map for cost surfaces.
records.evidence: There is no per-request cost to model: the site is static files served by GitHub Pages (free), dependencies load from unpkg (free CDN), and the codebase makes zero inference calls (system-map 'Models and providers': no in-product runtime model — so no inference line item). No server compute is billed per request, there is no tenancy, and there is no revenue path (README/disclaimer: 'historical/analytical synthesis, not investment advice; eToro links are navigation, not endorsement'). The one external API (bubblegauge.klee.me) runs and is billed as a separate service, not this repo, and is optional/gated. The A-31 hazard ('inference cost quietly destroying margin', 'an autonomous system finds the expensive path') cannot arise because this codebase performs no inference and provisions no metered compute.

residual_risk.tripwire: the A-25/A-28 'bgFetch is the only egress' test would flag introduction of a metered runtime call, at which point unit economics would become applicable

records.probe: Enumerate every import/require/install in the repo; for each package check existence, pinning (version+hash/lockfile), and whether an existence/registry gate blocks a fabricated or unpinned dep from reaching a build. Then check both the served CDN deps AND the CI harness deps.
records.evidence: SERVED SITE (strong): index.html loads 5 CDN scripts from unpkg, all exact-version-pinned (react 18.3.1, react-dom 18.3.1, prop-types 15.8.1, recharts 2.12.7, @babel/standalone 7.29.7) with sha384 integrity + crossorigin=anonymous. CI HARNESS (gap): verify/package.json declares `"playwright-core": "^1.40.0"` -- an UNPINNED caret range -- and there is NO committed lockfile (ls: verify/package-lock.json / npm-shrinkwrap.json both absent). verify.yml runs `npm install --no-audit --no-fund`, so the gate tooling resolves an unpinned dependency (and its transitive tree) at CI time with no hash pinning and no pre-install registry-existence gate. records.evidence: The maintenance agent has full repo write + push to main via the GitHub integration (system-map 'Tools the model can call': 'full repository write + push ... there is no policy bundle and no gate between an agent's output and production'; and 'The code-writing identity therefore has unrestricted write access to its own (nonexistent) gate' — the B-35 condition). .github/workflows/verify.yml runs on push but has `permissions: contents: read` and its header says it 'does not build or deploy it — Pages still serves the static files directly from main', so a failing gate does not stop the agent's push from reaching production. No branch protection could be confirmed (gh CLI unavailable: 'gh: command not found'); the audit baseline records none. The agent can edit the gate files themselves (verify/gate.js, verify/tests/*). gate.js merely *asserts* 'B-35 write-separation is a standing control' but that is a claim to test, not enforced least-privilege — no scoped identity, no sandbox, no immutable allowlist exists.

standing_control.mechanism: verify workflow runs on push but does not gate the Pages deploy; no branch protection / scoped identity / sandbox confirmed
standing_control.blocks: nothing (advisory only)
standing_control.ratchet: none

structural_fix.rationale: The A-11 tool-gateway that enforces per-agent scope at the door is not implemented. The gate exists as CI but is not wired to be the door — Pages deploys from main regardless of gate outcome, so least privilege remains a policy the agent is trusted to honour rather than a table enforced against it.

residual_risk.compensating_control: verify.yml runs the deterministic gate on every push (visible signal), and calibration catches 6/6 seeded defects — but it reports, it does not block the deploy.
residual_risk.tripwire: A push to main that fails the gate yet is live at crash.klee.me.

records.remediation: Separate the code-writing identity from the deploy/merge authority: enable branch protection on main requiring the verify check to pass and disallowing direct pushes; deploy Pages via an Actions workflow that runs the gate from a protected ref so a red gate blocks production; store the gate/allowlist where the writing agent has no write access (B-35). Grant the agent the minimum scopes and deny-by-default; verify by having it attempt an out-of-scope action on every build and requiring the attempt to fail.

records.probe: Asked what 'normal' looks like for the maintenance agent (commit/tool-call volume, tool mix, target hosts, data volume) and whether an anomaly triggers automatic containment. Searched the repo for an audit log of every tool call and any anomaly/kill-switch mechanism.
records.evidence: No per-agent behavioural baseline, anomaly detector, kill switch, or tool-call audit log exists. The data-stores table (system map) lists only client sessionStorage, an in-memory fetch cache, and git history — no agent-activity log. verify/ contains only static-content, golden-hash, supply-chain, SRI, and gate self-test suites (ls verify/tests); none observe or baseline agent runtime behaviour. There is therefore no way to distinguish 'an agent behaving anomalously' from 'an agent behaving', and nothing that would contain an anomaly automatically. The failure mode the check guards (aggregate-harmful behaviour from individually-legitimate tool calls) is completely unmonitored.

standing_control.mechanism: None — no agent baseline, anomaly detection, tool-call audit log, or automatic containment
standing_control.blocks: nothing
standing_control.ratchet: none

residual_risk.compensating_control: git history provides after-the-fact attribution of what an agent changed, and the golden-hash/static-security gates catch a defined class of harmful diffs at CI time.
residual_risk.tripwire: An agent session whose individual commits each pass the gate but whose aggregate behaviour (e.g. mass edits, new egress hosts) is harmful.

records.remediation: Start by logging every maintenance-agent tool call with arguments to an append-only store outside agent write; derive a per-agent baseline (commit cadence, files touched, hosts reached); wire anomaly detection to an automatic containment action (revoke the session token / block the push) rather than an alert. You cannot baseline what you do not record — logging is step one.

records.probe: Asked what 'output quality' means for this system and how the team would learn it degraded before users complain. Looked for a runtime model whose output could decay and for online evaluation / provider-change canaries.
records.evidence: The served site produces no model-generated output at runtime — all product content is frozen constants in dashboard.jsx (CRISES/MATRIX/CLASSIFICATION, system-map 'What this system is'). Those constants cannot 'decay in quality'; they can only be *changed by a commit*, and any such change is caught automatically by the golden-content hash test (verify/tests/20-golden-content.test.js: sha256 over the frozen data, goes RED on any number/string/structure change). The one model-authored datum, the external bubblegauge `judgment_call.text`, is consumed and rendered with explicit stale/error provenance labels (bubblegauge.jsx JudgmentInline; SCORE_FIXTURE.judgment_call has stale/error_class fields) — the site does not generate it and cannot evaluate its quality; that model belongs to the external service.

standing_control.mechanism: verify/tests/20-golden-content.test.js golden hash over frozen data (content integrity, the nearest analog to drift detection)
standing_control.blocks: a silent change to frozen content at CI
standing_control.ratchet: S11 — baseline change requires a decision record

residual_risk.compensating_control: The golden-content hash test detects any change to the frozen content at CI time (the integrity analog of drift detection); provenance labels flag stale/errored external text to the user.
residual_risk.tripwire: If the site ever generates output at runtime (e.g. summarizes the API text via a model), quality drift becomes real and this check reactivates.

history.reason: The NOT-APPLICABLE premise ("no runtime generative model whose output quality varies") is falsified by the engagement's own designated AI-adjacent surface: bubblegauge.jsx (gated on ?status-api=<key>). In JudgmentInline (bubblegauge.jsx:476-485) the external model-authored free-text field judgment_call.text is fetched live from bubblegauge.klee.me and rendered directly to end users, truncated to 2

records.na_justification: The check targets cross-environment credential bleed (staging/dev pointing at a prod DB, vector store, or API key). That capability does not exist in this architecture: there is exactly one environment (GitHub Pages serving branch main); the 'local preview' is python3 -m http.server over the identical static files with no credentials; and the entire codebase holds no connection strings, credentials, endpoints, or vector-store handles (grep-confirmed this run, corroborated by the audit surface and system map). There are no data planes to separate and no credential to be held by the wrong environment.
records.probe: Grepped the entire served codebase (index.html, dashboard.jsx, bubblegauge.jsx) for password/secret/api-key/connection-string/database/production-env identifiers, and read audit/00-audit-surface.json's identities/data_stores/configuration sections, hunting for a non-production environment holding a production credential, connection string, or vector-store handle.
records.evidence: grep -rniE 'password|secret|api[_-]?key|connection.?string|database|prod...' over the three served files returned ZERO matches. audit/00-audit-surface.json data_stores = only sessionStorage['bubblegauge:enabled'] (browser), an in-memory 25-min cache (browser), and git history; no server-side store, no DB, no vector store, no credential. bubblegauge.jsx:46-57 derives the API base purely from the URL query key (demo=>null, localhost=>http://localhost:8000, else https://<key>.<parent>) — there is no stored credential to leak. System map (audit/00-system-map.md:20-26) records exactly ONE environment (production = Pages/main); 'no staging environment' and 'holds no secrets, no credentials, no personal data'.

structural_fix.rationale: The S13 fix (an environment-scoped credential broker) presupposes credentials and multiple environments; neither exists. Making a non-production workload 'structurally incapable of holding a production credential' is moot when no credential exists anywhere and there is no non-production workload.

residual_risk.compensating_control: The single-environment / zero-credential property is itself asserted this run and is cheap to keep asserted; verify/tests/30-static-security.test.js already scans the served files for dangerous constructs and could be extended to fail if any credential/connection-string literal is ever introduced.
residual_risk.tripwire: A future feature that introduces a stored secret or a second (staging) environment would re-open this check; a grep-based no-credential assertion in the CI suite would fire on the introducing commit.

records.probe: Read bubblegauge.jsx activation/gating path and searched for any tripwire that flips the feature off automatically on a signal (error rate, cost, guardrail, eval score); checked verify/tests for a test that exercises the switch on a schedule.
records.evidence: bubblegauge.jsx:26-64 gates the ENTIRE AI-adjacent feature on the ?status-api=<key> query param and persists it in sessionStorage['bubblegauge:enabled']; with no param window.BubbleGauge={enabled:false} (line 64) and the feature no-ops — release is genuinely decoupled from deploy and fails closed. ?status-api-off clears activation (audit-surface route). DEMO_KEYS={demo,fixture} (line 27) give a pre-verified offline safe mode, and per-item degradation exists ('suppressed (block degraded)', stale/fallback_used/dropped labels, lines 95/118/197/208-210). verify/tests/30-static-security.test.js:92-93 asserts BG defaults to {enabled:false} when the module is absent — a standing test of the fail-closed default. BUT: grep for chaos/inject/tripwire/automatic-flip found none; the switch is per-visitor and manual (URL/sessionStorage), there is no automatic tripwire wired to any signal, and no monthly kill-switch drill.

standing_control.mechanism: verify/tests/30-static-security.test.js asserts the fail-closed default (BG={enabled:false}) and the query-param gate; the feature is decoupled from deploy and off by default.
standing_control.blocks: It asserts the no-op default holds, but does not block release of an AI feature lacking an automatic tripwire.
standing_control.cadence: on every push/PR (verify.yml), though the gate is non-blocking for the Pages deploy at baseline.
standing_control.ratchet: none for automatic-tripwire coverage — no test exercises the switch on a schedule or verifies auto-flip.

records.remediation: The feature is off-by-default and per-visitor, so a global 'misbehaving feature' blast radius is bounded — but wire an automatic disable: since the only runtime signal available client-side is the fetch layer's own error/stale rate, have bubblegauge trip itself to demo/suppressed mode after N consecutive API errors or a stale-age threshold, and add a verify/ test that injects an erroring API stub and asserts the feature auto-degrades. That converts the existing manual off-switch into a tested, self-firing kill switch.

records.probe: Read index.html SRI declarations and verify/tests/40-sri-recompute.test.js + 20-golden-content.test.js to see what artifact integrity is verified and whether verification fails closed AT DEPLOY; checked verify.yml + gate.js + audit surface to see whether the gate actually blocks a push-to-main deploy.
records.evidence: Dependency artifacts ARE integrity-verified: index.html carries sha384 SRI on all 5 CDN scripts, the browser verify-on-load fails closed (refuses mismatched scripts), and verify/tests/40-sri-recompute.test.js:32-53 re-derives sha384 from fetched bytes and asserts it matches, expecting exactly 5 SRI'd deps (line 37-38); verify/tests/35-supply-chain pins versions. The site's OWN artifacts have a golden integrity hash: tests/20-golden-content.test.js:16-22 hashes the frozen content and compares to golden/data-hash.json. HOWEVER gate.js is a CI-only signal — its header (lines 3-11) says it makes 'the deploy decision by CODE' but audit/00-audit-surface.json:45 records branch protection on main = NONE and system map:22 says any push to main is production with 'no admission control of any kind'. So verify-on-DEPLOY that refuses a modified artifact does not exist for the app's own files: a modified dashboard.jsx that breaks the golden hash still deploys to Pages. No cryptographic signing (cosign/Sigstore) of the site artifacts; builds are trivially reproducible (there is no build).

standing_control.mechanism: SRI verify-on-load (browser, fails closed) for CDN artifacts, recomputed by verify/tests/40-sri-recompute.test.js; golden content-hash over the site's own frozen files (tests/20-golden-content.test.js).
standing_control.blocks: For CDN deps: browser execution of a tampered script (real, fail-closed). For the site's own artifacts: the CI gate flags a hash change but does NOT block the Pages deploy — no branch protection.
standing_control.cadence: every push/PR via verify.yml; browser SRI on every page view.
standing_control.ratchet: golden hash is a ratchet against silent content change in CI, but non-gating for production at baseline.

records.remediation: Wire the existing gate to actually block deploy: enable branch protection on main requiring the verify workflow (golden-content + SRI-recompute + supply-chain) to pass before merge, so a modified/unverified artifact is refused rather than served. That upgrades the golden hash from advisory characterization to fail-closed verify-on-deploy. Signing (cosign) is optional given a static repo, but the blocking gate is the load-bearing fix — without it the golden hash is theatre with no audience.

records.probe: Searched the repo for any production-failure detection (liveness/readiness, synthetic monitoring of crash.klee.me, uptime check, anomaly detection) and any automated response bound to an alert class; inspected verify.yml (the only automation) to see if it monitors production or only lint-checks code pre-merge.
records.evidence: audit/00-audit-surface.json scheduled_jobs=[], queues=[], pipelines=[] — no monitoring job of any kind. The only automation is .github/workflows/verify.yml, which runs node run.js / node gate.js on push/PR against the SOURCE; it performs static checks (data invariants, golden hash, SRI, supply-chain, governance) and never probes the live site crash.klee.me, has no liveness/readiness, no synthetic journey, no anomaly detection, and triggers no automated remediation. There is no alert class with an executing response anywhere in the repo. Production-failure detection→action coverage = 0/0; the undeclared dependency is total: if Pages serves a broken deploy or a CDN 404s, nothing notices and nothing responds.

residual_risk.compensating_control: GitHub Pages platform uptime is Microsoft/GitHub-managed; browser SRI fails closed on a tampered CDN script (so a supply-chain swap is refused client-side, not merely detected). Neither detects a broken deploy or content regression.
residual_risk.tripwire: None automated — a production outage or content regression is currently invisible until a human visits the site.

records.remediation: Add a scheduled synthetic monitor (GitHub Actions cron, out-of-band) that GETs https://crash.klee.me, asserts HTTP 200 + a known content marker + that the 5 SRI scripts resolve, and on failure fires an AUTOMATED response — e.g. open/label a tracking issue and auto-revert main to the last verified commit (git revert + push) rather than only paging. Define an MTTD target. Break-glass human escalation is the backstop (S10), not the control.

records.probe: Read bubblegauge.jsx's fetch/degradation layer for reliability primitives on the one external-dependency critical path, then grepped verify/tests for any failure-injection / chaos test that actually breaks a dependency and asserts graceful degradation.
records.evidence: Reliability primitives DO exist on the only critical dependency (the bubblegauge API): timeouts (tests/40 uses req.setTimeout; bubblegauge has a fetch timeout layer), a 25-min in-memory cache TTL, and documented degradation contract 'Service never returns 500 on upstream failure: fallback or drop+renormalize' (bubblegauge.jsx:189), plus per-item states 'suppressed (block degraded)' (line 95/118), stale/fallback_used/dropped flags (208-210), and an offline demo/fixture path (27,204-237). BUT these are DESIGNED, not VALIDATED by injection: grep for chaos/inject/failure over verify/tests found only a fetch-timeout in the SRI test's own network helper and a static assertion (tests/30-static-security.test.js:66) that catch handlers do not fake-2xx — no test kills the API, adds latency, or returns 500 and asserts the strip degrades gracefully. No scheduled chaos experiment, no steady-state hypothesis, no experiment set.

standing_control.mechanism: Graceful-degradation primitives are present in bubblegauge.jsx, but no automated, recurring failure-injection experiment validates them.
standing_control.blocks: nothing — no chaos experiment gates release.
standing_control.ratchet: none — graceful-degradation success rate is neither measured nor ratcheted.

records.remediation: Add a failure-injection test to verify/ that stubs the bubblegauge API to (a) time out, (b) return 500, (c) return partial/stale payloads, and asserts the rendered strip degrades to suppressed/stale/demo without throwing and without fabricating a score — run it in verify.yml on every push (gating) so the experiment set is exercised automatically. New external dependencies should be added to that stub set. The experiment is the evidence, not the primitives.

records.na_justification: The check requires load-testing owned compute to its knee and bounding owned inference capacity. This system owns neither: it is static assets on a platform-managed CDN with no server-side compute and no in-product model (system map:29). There is no autoscaling knob, no connection pool, and no inference quota this repo controls; the only inference-adjacent path consumes an external service's capacity. GitHub Pages capacity/scaling is platform-managed and not a parameter of this codebase.
records.probe: Checked the architecture for any server-side compute, autoscaling policy, or in-product inference whose capacity could be load-tested to a knee, and for any inference quota this system owns.
records.evidence: audit/00-system-map.md:29 'In-product runtime models: none. No inference call is made by this codebase.' The product is static files served by GitHub Pages' CDN (system map:9-13); there is no server, no container, no autoscaler, no connection pool this repo controls (audit-surface: no pipelines, no server-side data_stores). The bubblegauge integration only GET-consumes text from an external service (bubblegauge.klee.me) whose capacity belongs to that separate service, not this system. Client rendering runs on the visitor's own browser. There is no inference quota, rate limit, or hardware constraint owned here to plan or bound.

residual_risk.compensating_control: GitHub Pages CDN absorbs static-asset load; there is no marginal per-request cost to this repo and no autoscaler to be weaponized (so the B-08 denial-of-wallet interaction is inert here — no cost meter to run away).
residual_risk.tripwire: GitHub Pages soft bandwidth limits (~100GB/mo) are platform-enforced and out-of-repo; unmonitored, but breaching them throttles rather than bills. If in-product inference is ever added, this check re-applies immediately.

records.probe: Identified every stateful store in the architecture, determined the recovery mechanism, and checked for a tested/scheduled restore drill with recorded RPO/RTO.
records.evidence: audit/00-audit-surface.json data_stores and system map:48 establish the ONLY persistence is git history on GitHub ('the sole persistence + de-facto backup'); the two browser stores (sessionStorage key, in-memory cache) are ephemeral client state, not server data. Recovery is inherent and trivially exercisable: GitHub Pages redeploys the whole site from main on any push, and every git clone is a complete replica (this working tree IS such a clone at commit d19556d). BUT there is no scheduled, timed restore drill, no clean-environment restore test, and no documented RPO/RTO as a standing control — audit-surface scheduled_jobs=[] and no DR runbook exists in the repo. 'When did we last verify recovery' has no timestamp answer.

standing_control.mechanism: Git/GitHub provides inherent distributed replication (every clone is a full backup) and Pages auto-redeploys from main, so recoverability exists in practice — but no automated restore drill, timing, or RPO/RTO documentation makes it a monitored control.
standing_control.blocks: nothing
standing_control.ratchet: none — restore time is unmeasured, so it cannot be held from regressing.

records.remediation: Add a scheduled (e.g. weekly) CI job that clones the repo fresh into a clean runner, serves it (python3 -m http.server), smoke-tests that index.html renders and the 5 SRI scripts resolve, and records the wall-clock duration as the RTO signal; document RPO = last push to main and RTO = measured restore time, and make an overdue drill block release. This turns inherent git recoverability into a verified, timestamped control. Mitigation is cheap because there is no mutable data to reconcile — only source.

records.probe: Distinguished the layers of 'running infrastructure', asked for each whether a by-hand change would be noticed/reverted: (a) served content, (b) out-of-repo repo/Pages/DNS settings; searched for any reconciliation loop or drift detector.
records.evidence: Served-content layer is structurally reconciliation-free-of-drift: GitHub Pages serves branch main directly (system map:22, audit-surface configuration), so there is no separate deploy artifact that can diverge from the declared desired state (main) — the CNAME custom-domain declaration is also in-repo. BUT the infrastructure CONFIG layer is undeclared-as-code and undetected: audit/00-audit-surface.json:42-46 lists 'GitHub Pages: deploy from branch main / root' and 'custom domain crash.klee.me' as out-of-repo settings, and 'branch protection on main — state_at_freeze: NONE'. A hand change to the Pages source branch, a toggle of branch protection, or a DNS/CNAME change at the registrar would be noticed by nothing — there is no IaC, no reconciliation loop, no drift detector (scheduled_jobs=[]).

standing_control.mechanism: For served content only: GitHub Pages serving main directly means the deployed artifact IS the declared state — no content drift is possible without a commit to main.
standing_control.blocks: content divergence from main (structurally). Does NOT cover out-of-repo settings (Pages source, branch protection, DNS), which have no reconciliation.
standing_control.cadence: continuous for content (Pages redeploys from main on push).
standing_control.ratchet: none for the config layer — out-of-band settings changes are unreverted and undetected.

records.remediation: Encode the repo/Pages settings as code or add a scheduled CI assertion (via the GitHub API) that Pages source == main/root, branch protection is present, and the live CNAME/DNS resolves to crash.klee.me — alert and, where possible, auto-revert on divergence. The served-content layer needs nothing (it cannot drift from main), so this is bounded to the small out-of-repo config surface.

records.na_justification: The system has no retrieval-augmented pipeline: no embeddings, no vector store, no index, and no ingest path. The one persistent content store is the frozen data in dashboard.jsx, which is source code, not a retrieval corpus — and its integrity IS held by a standing control (verify/tests/20-golden-content.test.js: a sha256 golden hash over CRISES/MATRIX/CLASSIFICATION that goes red on any tamper, baseline verify/golden/data-hash.json). The check's named risks (embedding/index freshness, vector poisoning, provenance-at-ingest) address a capability that does not exist here, so it is architecturally N/A rather than a failed control.
records.probe: grep -riE 'embedding|vector|retrieval|rag|corpus|index.*freshness' over index.html, dashboard.jsx, bubblegauge.jsx — searched for any retrieval store, embedding index, or ingest path whose contents could become another user's retrieved context.
records.evidence: grep for embedding/vector/retrieval/RAG/corpus returned ZERO hits across all product source (this run). System map: all crisis content are frozen constants in dashboard.jsx (CRISES/MATRIX/CLASSIFICATION); no server, no database, no vector store, no embeddings. The only network read is bubblegauge.jsx bgFetch() GET-ing an external status API (lines 292-308), whose response lands in a per-tab in-memory `cache` object (line 289, 25-min TTL) that is never persisted, never shared between users, and never ingested into an index. There is no write path into any persistent retrieval corpus and no cross-user contamination channel.

records.na_justification: Time-to-first-token and inter-token latency are properties of a generative model producing tokens. This site makes no inference call at runtime (system map), so there is no TTFT to budget and no p95/p99 generation latency to track. The check targets a capability the architecture does not contain.
records.probe: grep -riE 'inference|llm|completion|token|latency|time-to-first-token' over product source, and read bubblegauge.jsx fetch layer — searched for any inference call whose TTFT / end-to-end latency could be measured.
records.evidence: grep for inference/model/token in bubblegauge.jsx + dashboard.jsx + index.html returned no runtime-model hits (only a CSS 'tokens' comment). System map §Models: 'In-product runtime models: none. No inference call is made by this codebase.' The only latency-bearing network op is bgFetch (bubblegauge.jsx:292-308), a plain GET against an external status API guarded by a 6s AbortController timeout — a data fetch, not token generation. There is no streamed generation, no first-token event, no model gateway.

records.probe: Established who/what can write to production and whether the code-writing identity can also rewrite the gate that judges it. Checked credentials, not intent: read git config identity, the remote, the location of the policy bundle relative to the code, the workflow permissions, and the branch-protection state at freeze.
records.evidence: The policy bundle is co-located with the code it gates, under ONE identity: verify/gate.js, verify/tests/*.test.js, and .github/workflows/verify.yml all live in the SAME repo (mglaeser/save-haven-monitor) as dashboard.jsx/bubblegauge.jsx. git config shows the code-writing agent commits as `Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>`; `git log` confirms that identity authored both the product code (82565b6) and the entire gate (4af8354). There is no separate policy repository and no separate write credential — grep for 'separate repo/policy repo' finds the requirement only in the catalogue text, never in an implementation. Therefore the agent that writes the code can, in the same push, rewrite gate.js AND its own self-test (tests/50-gate-selftest.test.js) — the author controls the gate. Worse, the gate does not even block: audit/00-audit-surface.json:45 records `branch protection on main … state_at_freeze: NONE`, and DR-002-ci-gate.md admits R-GATE: 'Until branch protection requires the verify check on main, the gate is advisory, not blocking.' gate.js:13 CLAIMS 'B-35 write-separation is a standing control' — under Rule 2 that comment is material to test, not evidence; the credential reality falsifies it.

standing_control.mechanism: No continuous assertion that no identity holds both code-write and policy-write exists — it cannot, because there is only one repo and one write identity. verify/tests/60-governance.test.js asserts the workflow is wired and 50-gate-selftest asserts the gate blocks, but both are editable by the same agent in the same commit and neither enforces credential separation.
standing_control.blocks: Nothing in the segregation sense: at freeze the workflow is advisory (no branch protection), and the gate/tests are writable by the author they judge.
standing_control.ratchet: none for write-separation

structural_fix.rationale: S13: move the policy bundle (gate + tests + golden baselines + workflow) into its own repository with its own write identity and its own pipeline, so segregation becomes a fact about which key holds which permission rather than a comment. Not taken — verify/ is inside the gated repo under the same identity.

residual_risk.compensating_control: gate.js is self-tested (50-gate-selftest) and the wiring is tripwired (60-governance); both run in CI on the branch. This narrows accidental gate removal but does NOT stop a deliberate same-identity edit.
residual_risk.tripwire: verify/tests/60-governance.test.js goes red if the workflow is deleted/decoupled — but an agent can edit the tripwire too.

records.remediation: Two moves, structural first: (1) Extract the policy bundle (verify/gate.js, verify/tests/, verify/golden/, .github/workflows/verify.yml) into a separate repo with a distinct write credential that NO code-writing agent identity holds; have the product repo's CI pull the policy bundle read-only and fail closed if it cannot. (2) Until then, the owner must enable branch protection on main requiring the `verify` check with 'do not allow bypassing' (per DR-002 R-GATE), so the advisory gate becomes blocking. Note the mandate escalation: a breach of this control is a STOP-SHIP — an author who can edit its own gate has no gate.

records.probe: Listed every 'model version in use' and asked what happens at its end-of-life. grep -riE 'model|gpt-|anthropic|deprecat' over product source; read the pinned-dependency inventory and the supply-chain test.
records.evidence: grep for runtime model references in product source returns none; system map §Models confirms 'In-product runtime models: none.' The only pinned external artifacts are CDN library versions (react/react-dom 18.3.1, prop-types 15.8.1, recharts 2.12.7, @babel/standalone 7.29.7) declared in index.html, and these ARE inventoried and enforced (verify/golden/pinned-deps.json + verify/tests/35-supply-chain.test.js, which fails on any silent version bump). No LLM/model with a provider deprecation window sits in the production path. The maintenance-time Claude agents are the authors, not a runtime dependency the served site would go dark without.

history.reason: NOT-APPLICABLE is over-scoped to a runtime *product* model. The audited architecture does contain a deprecation-exposed model: the maintenance-time Anthropic model (`claude-fable-5`), which the system map lists under "Models and providers" as the sole production path (agent push = production) and explicitly flags with "No pinning, no provenance recording, and no second-vendor verifier exists at ba

records.na_justification: Right-to-erasure requires personal data held in server-side or derived stores. This system collects and stores no personal data anywhere it controls (system map; CLAUDE.md no-analytics/no-tracker policy). The only client-side value is a non-personal activation key in the user's own sessionStorage, fully under the user's control. With no personal-data store and no derived stores, the erasure-test control governs a capability the architecture does not contain.
records.probe: Picked a hypothetical user and traced every store their data could reach: grep -riE 'email|password|cookie|localStorage|personal|user id|analytics|tracking' over product source; enumerated the data stores from the system map.
records.evidence: grep for PII/tracking returns no user-data handling (dashboard hits are research prose about market 'liquidity', not user records; sessionStorage usage in bubblegauge.jsx:35-42 stores only the activation KEY string `bubblegauge:enabled`, in the visitor's own browser, which they clear at will via ?status-api-off). System map §Data stores lists exactly three: client sessionStorage (one activation key), an in-memory fetch cache, and git history — 'No server-side stores, no databases, no vector stores … no user data, no analytics or trackers (by CLAUDE.md policy).' There is no primary store, backup, prompt log, trace store, tuning set, or embedding derived from personal data — so there is nothing to erase and no derived-store fan-out to prove clean.

records.na_justification: Inference economics (prompt-cache hit rate, cost-based model routing, cost per unit of value) presuppose paid model calls. This codebase makes none at runtime (system map). The check targets a cost surface the architecture does not have.
records.probe: Attempted to measure prompt-cache hit rate and cost split across models: grep -riE 'inference|prompt|cache.*hit|cost|model rout' over product source; inspected the only cache in the codebase.
records.evidence: The only 'cache' in the product is bubblegauge.jsx's in-memory HTTP response cache (line 289-290, `const cache = {}; const TTL = 25*60*1000`) — a data-fetch cache with no relation to prompt caching or per-token cost. grep finds no inference, no prompt, no model routing, no billing surface. System map confirms zero runtime inference. There is no model invocation whose cost-per-unit-of-value could be measured, no prompt prefix to keep cacheable, and no invoice to watch.

records.probe: Asked which named reference framework the platform was designed against, and whether that review is a held, re-run control or a one-off document. Read the audit apparatus (catalogue, findings, gate) and searched CLAUDE.md/README for a design framework.
records.evidence: At the frozen baseline the atlas was built ad hoc by agents — no reference framework (twelve-factor, well-architected, generative-AI/responsible-AI lenses) is named in CLAUDE.md, README.md, or INTEGRATION_NOTES.md for the product's design. Retroactively, this engagement DOES apply a named framework — the Due-Diligence and Remediation Mandate catalogue (audit/00-check-catalogue.json, 119 checks / 79 active), with gaps recorded as findings (audit/03-findings.json) rather than dropped, and findings-file integrity enforced deterministically by verify/gate.js (§2: exactly the active check IDs, no PASS with null standing_control). So the 'gaps tracked as findings with owning roles' half of the standing control genuinely exists and is machine-held. What is missing is the cadence: no mechanism re-runs the framework review on a schedule or on major architecture change — the audit is a one-time engagement, and the well-architected/twelve-factor AI lenses were never applied to the design as such.

standing_control.mechanism: The mandate catalogue is applied as the reference framework; gaps are recorded in audit/03-findings.json and their existence + integrity is enforced on every pipeline run by verify/gate.js (findings count must equal active checks; no PASS without a standing_control).
standing_control.blocks: A merge whose findings file drops or mis-registers an active check (gate.js fails closed) — but only advisory until branch protection is on (see B-35 R-GATE).
standing_control.cadence: per-push integrity check only; no scheduled framework re-run
standing_control.ratchet: S11 partial: open framework gaps are the findings backlog; the gate prevents silently deleting a finding record, so gaps cannot be quietly dropped.

residual_risk.compensating_control: gate.js findings-integrity keeps the gap list honest between reviews.
residual_risk.tripwire: verify/tests/60-governance.test.js red if audit artifacts removed.

records.remediation: Name the framework(s) explicitly in a design record and apply the AI-specific lenses to the actual architecture (for a static zero-build site: the generative/responsible-AI lens over the sole AI-adjacent surface bubblegauge.jsx, plus the relevant well-architected pillars — security, reliability of the CDN/egress paths). Then make the review a held control: a scheduled/on-architecture-change re-run that regenerates the gap list, with each open gap carrying an owning role, so 'framework adherence' is a recurring assertion rather than this one engagement's snapshot.
